column1: Parables and Stories in the Quran

column5: Description
The creation of Adam from clay, his elevation above angels, Iblis's refusal to prostrate, temptation in Paradise, and the fall to Earth.
Owners of a garden planned to harvest without sharing with the poor; their garden was destroyed overnight. A parable about greed and charity.

column1: The People of the Cave (Seven Sleepers)
column5: Young men flee persecution for their faith and take refuge in a cave where Allah causes them to sleep for 309 years.
Noah preaches to his people for 950 years, they reject him, Allah commands him to build an ark, the flood comes, and believers are saved.

column1: The People of 'Ad and Prophet Hud
column5: The people of 'Ad were powerful but arrogant. Prophet Hud called them to Allah, but they rejected him and were destroyed by a violent wind.

column1: The People of Thamud and Prophet Salih
column5: Salih was sent to Thamud who demanded a miracle; a she-camel emerged from rock but they killed it and were destroyed by an earthquake/thunderbolt.

column4: Abraham, Nimrod, Sarah, Hagar, Ishmael, Isaac
column5: Abraham's search for truth, breaking idols, being thrown in fire but saved, sacrificing his son, building the Ka'bah, and being the patriarch of monotheism.
Lot preached against homosexuality and immorality; his people rejected him and were destroyed by a rain of stones, while he and believers were saved.

column1: The People of Madyan and Prophet Shu'ayb
column5: Shu'ayb called his people to stop cheating in trade and economic injustice; they were destroyed by an earthquake/thunderbolt.

column4: Joseph, Jacob, Joseph's brothers, Aziz, Aziz's wife
column5: The most complete narrative in a single surah, covering Joseph's dream, betrayal by brothers, slavery in Egypt, temptation, imprisonment, rise to power, and family reunion.

column4: Moses, Aaron, Pharaoh, Children of Israel
column5: Moses' birth, upbringing in Pharaoh's palace, exile to Madyan, calling at Mount Tur, confrontation with Pharaoh, exodus, and receiving the Torah.
Children of Israel were ordered to sacrifice a cow with specific attributes to solve a murder case; the dead man was struck with part of the sacrificed cow and revived to identify his killer.
Qarun was extremely wealthy from Moses' people but became arrogant. He was swallowed by the earth along with his treasures as punishment.
The tyranny of Pharaoh, his claim to divinity, persecution of Children of Israel, rejection of Moses, plagues, and drowning in the Red Sea.
David defeated Goliath, was given kingdom and wisdom, mountains and birds glorified Allah with him, and he was skilled in making armor.

column4: Solomon, Queen of Sheba, Jinns, Hoopoe bird
column5: Solomon inherited David's kingdom, understood animal language, commanded wind and jinns, and had interaction with the Queen of Sheba who eventually embraced monotheism.
Abraha, the Abyssinian ruler of Yemen, marched with elephants to destroy the Ka'bah but was defeated by birds dropping stones of baked clay.
Jonah left his people in anger, was swallowed by a whale, glorified Allah from its belly, was forgiven and cast ashore, and his people ultimately believed.
Job was afflicted with disease and loss of wealth and family but remained patient. He eventually called to Allah who restored his health, family, and wealth.
A righteous king who traveled to the far west and east, establishing justice, and built a barrier between two mountains to protect people from Gog and Magog.
A man (identified in tradition as Uzair) passed by a ruined town and wondered how Allah would resurrect it. Allah caused him to die for 100 years, then revived him to witness resurrection firsthand.

column1: The Parable of the Two Gardens
column5: Two men, one with two lush gardens who became arrogant and ungrateful, and another who reminded him of Allah. The ungrateful man's gardens were destroyed.
Allah is described as the Light of the heavens and earth, likened to a lamp in a glass, the glass like a brilliant star, lit from a blessed olive tree.

column4: Messengers, a man from the farthest part of city
column5: Three messengers were sent to a town; people rejected them; a believing man from the outskirts came to support the messengers but was killed; the town was destroyed.
Those who take protectors other than Allah are likened to a spider taking a web as a home, which is the weakest of homes.
A man given Allah's signs but who rejected them is compared to a dog that pants whether left alone or driven away.
Maryam's birth, dedication to temple, miraculous sustenance, angelic visitation, virgin birth of Jesus, and defense against accusations.
Jesus' miraculous birth, speaking from the cradle, performing miracles, gathering disciples, ascension to heaven, and future return.

column1: The Story of Zakariya and Yahya
column5: Zakariya's old-age prayer for an heir, miraculous birth of Yahya, and Yahya's righteousness and prophethood.

column1: The Parable of the Good Word
column5: A good word is compared to a good tree with firm roots and branches reaching to the sky, bearing fruit in all seasons.
People who violated the Sabbath by fishing when prohibited were transformed into apes as punishment for their transgression.
Those who were entrusted with the Torah but failed to uphold it are likened to a donkey carrying books without understanding them.

column1: The Parable of the Blind and Deaf
column5: One who was spiritually dead and given life (faith) is contrasted with one walking in darkness, like comparing the seeing to the blind.

column1: The Story of the People of the Ditch
column5: A tyrannical king dug ditches of fire to burn believers who refused to renounce their faith.
A wise man given wisdom by Allah who advised his son about monotheism, prayer, patience, and good conduct.

column2: - Triple goddesses (e.g., Fates, Norns, Hinds) symbolizing fate, nature, or sovereignty.
column3: - Al-Lat, Al-Uzza, Manat (53:19-20) – Pre-Islamic "daughters of Allah" condemned as false gods.
column4: The Quran rejects the "three sisters" as idols but acknowledges their existence in Arabian paganism.

column2: - Hero must pass three tests (e.g., Hercules’ labors, Odin’s sacrifices).
column3:
1. Abraham’s trials:
- Rejection by his people (37:83-98)
- Command to sacrifice his son (37:100-107)
- Building the Kaaba (2:127).
2. Moses’ trials:
- Confronting Pharaoh (20:45-79)
- The Golden Calf (20:85-97)
- Rebellion of Korah (28:76-82).
3. Job’s suffering (38:41-44).
column4: The Quranic trials emphasize faith in God rather than heroic feats.

column2: - PIE gods often appear in threes (e.g., Trimurti, Zeus-Poseidon-Hades).
column3:
- Explicit rejection of the Trinity (5:73): "Do not say 'Three'... Allah is but One God."
- Condemnation of the three Meccan goddesses (53:19-23).
column4: The Quran denies triadic divinity but acknowledges its cultural presence.

column2: - Indra vs. Vritra, Thor vs. Jörmungandr – chaos monsters defeated.
column3:
- Dhu al-Qarnayn vs. Gog and Magog (18:94-98) – a barrier contains chaotic forces.
- Pharaoh as a tyrant (28:4) but no direct dragon battle.
column4: The Quran uses metaphorical chaos figures rather than literal dragons.

column3:
- Jesus’ revival of the dead (3:49, 5:110) – but not a resurrection myth.
- Day of Judgment (75:3-4) – universal resurrection.
column4: The Quran focuses on linear eschatology, not cyclical returns.

column2: - Primordial cow in PIE myths (e.g., Audumla in Norse, Kamadhenu in Hindu).
column3: - The Cow of the Israelites (2:67-73) – a test of faith, not a divine being.
column4: The Quranic cow is a ritual object, not a cosmic entity.

column8: Other Influences
column9: Notable Discrepancies

column5: The creation of Adam from clay, his elevation above angels, Iblis's refusal to prostrate, temptation in Paradise, and the fall to Earth.
column6: Possible elements in Iblis's refusal to bow (comparable to Ahriman in dualistic cosmology)
column8: Some elements may reflect Talmudic and Midrashic traditions
column9: In Quran: Angels prostrate to Adam; Satan/Iblis refuses out of pride not jealousy; no apple mentioned; both Adam and Eve are equally blamed; no curse of pain in childbirth

column5: Owners of a garden planned to harvest without sharing with the poor; their garden was destroyed overnight. A parable about greed and charity.
column7: Similar themes in Biblical parables (Luke 12:16-21 - Rich Fool)
column8: May reflect Arabian moral traditions about charity
column9: Unique to Quranic narrative

column1: The People of the Cave (Seven Sleepers)
column5: Young men flee persecution for their faith and take refuge in a cave where Allah causes them to sleep for 309 years.
column8: Christian tradition: "Seven Sleepers of Ephesus"; may reflect Eastern Christian traditions circulating in Near East
column9: Quran is ambiguous about number (mentions "some say 3, others 5, others 7"); includes a dog; specifies 309 years of sleep

column5: Noah preaches to his people for 950 years, they reject him, Allah commands him to build an ark, the flood comes, and believers are saved.
column6: Possible parallels with Yima's vara (enclosure) in Avesta
column8: Mesopotamian flood stories (Epic of Gilgamesh, Atrahasis)
column9: Quran: Noah's son refuses to board ark and drowns; emphasis on Noah as prophet not just righteous man; flood presented as local punishment; no mention of rainbow covenant

column1: The People of 'Ad and Prophet Hud
column5: The people of 'Ad were powerful but arrogant. Prophet Hud called them to Allah, but they rejected him and were destroyed by a violent wind.
column7: No direct Biblical parallel; some thematic similarity to Tower of Babel
column8: May reflect pre-Islamic Arabian historical traditions of lost tribes
column9: Unique to Quranic tradition; Hud not mentioned in Bible or other ancient texts

column1: The People of Thamud and Prophet Salih
column5: Salih was sent to Thamud who demanded a miracle; a she-camel emerged from rock but they killed it and were destroyed by an earthquake/thunderbolt.
column7: Thamud briefly mentioned in extra-Biblical sources; no Biblical parallel for Salih
column8: Historical Thamudic civilization known in Arabian peninsula
column9: Unique to Quranic narrative; archaeological evidence confirms Thamudic civilization existed

column4: Abraham, Nimrod, Sarah, Hagar, Ishmael, Isaac
column5: Abraham's search for truth, breaking idols, being thrown in fire but saved, sacrificing his son, building the Ka'bah, and being the patriarch of monotheism.
column8: Talmudic and Midrashic accounts about idol-breaking, Nimrod
column9: Quran: Ambiguous about which son was to be sacrificed; emphasis on Abraham's discovery of monotheism through observation; building Ka'bah with Ishmael in Mecca; direct confrontation with father over idolatry

column5: Lot preached against homosexuality and immorality; his people rejected him and were destroyed by a rain of stones, while he and believers were saved.
column8: May reflect broader Near Eastern traditions
column9: Quran: Lot portrayed as prophet rather than just Abraham's nephew; more emphasis on Lot's preaching; wife's betrayal more explicit than merely looking back; angels appear as young men but no attempted assault by townspeople

column1: The People of Madyan and Prophet Shu'ayb
column5: Shu'ayb called his people to stop cheating in trade and economic injustice; they were destroyed by an earthquake/thunderbolt.
column7: Midian in Bible (Exodus); Jethro, Moses' father-in-law
column8: May reflect historical Midianite civilization
column9: Quran: Shu'ayb portrayed as prophet to Midian with emphasis on economic justice; traditionally identified with Jethro though not explicitly stated in Quran

column4: Joseph, Jacob, Joseph's brothers, Aziz, Aziz's wife
column5: The most complete narrative in a single surah, covering Joseph's dream, betrayal by brothers, slavery in Egypt, temptation, imprisonment, rise to power, and family reunion.
column8: Possibly influenced by "Qisas al-Anbiya" (Stories of the Prophets) traditions
column9: Quran: More details about wife's attempted seduction and Egyptian women cutting hands; prison companion's dreams more detailed; explicit acknowledgment of Joseph's beauty; no mention of blessing given to Judah

column4: Moses, Aaron, Pharaoh, Children of Israel
column5: Moses' birth, upbringing in Pharaoh's palace, exile to Madyan, calling at Mount Tur, confrontation with Pharaoh, exodus, and receiving the Torah.
column6: Possible elements in the encounter with fire
column8: Midrashic accounts; Egyptian historical elements
column9: Quran: Moses more reluctant in mission; more dialogue with Pharaoh; specific magicians who convert; Pharaoh's claimed divinity emphasized; Moses' speech impediment mentioned but Aaron not designated as primary speaker; Pharaoh's repentance attempt at drowning

column5: Children of Israel were ordered to sacrifice a cow with specific attributes to solve a murder case; the dead man was struck with part of the sacrificed cow and revived to identify his killer.
column8: May reflect Midrashic elaborations
column9: Quran: Links cow sacrifice directly to solving a murder; resurrection element absent from Biblical account; strong emphasis on Israelites' reluctance and repeated questioning

column5: Qarun was extremely wealthy from Moses' people but became arrogant. He was swallowed by the earth along with his treasures as punishment.
column8: May reflect Talmudic elaborations on Korah's wealth
column9: Quran: Emphasis on extreme wealth and arrogance; portrayed as economic oppressor; no mention of priestly leadership dispute from Biblical account; presented as individual rebellion rather than group conspiracy

column5: The tyranny of Pharaoh, his claim to divinity, persecution of Children of Israel, rejection of Moses, plagues, and drowning in the Red Sea.
column8: Egyptian historical elements
column9: Quran: Includes Haman as Pharaoh's minister (name from later Biblical period); mentions preservation of Pharaoh's body as sign; fewer plagues described; more emphasis on Pharaoh's claim of divinity; presents believer from Pharaoh's family who defends Moses

column5: David defeated Goliath, was given kingdom and wisdom, mountains and birds glorified Allah with him, and he was skilled in making armor.
column8: May reflect Talmudic traditions about David
column9: Quran: Less detail about David's life; mountains and birds praised with him; given skill with iron; judicial case differs from Biblical Bathsheba narrative; milder treatment of David's error

column4: Solomon, Queen of Sheba, Jinns, Hoopoe bird
column5: Solomon inherited David's kingdom, understood animal language, commanded wind and jinns, and had interaction with the Queen of Sheba who eventually embraced monotheism.
column6: Possible elements in control over supernatural beings
column8: Talmudic traditions; Ethiopian Kebra Nagast
column9: Quran: More supernatural elements (control of wind, jinn); communication with animals; Queen of Sheba converts to monotheism; detailed account of hoopoe bird as messenger; no romantic relationship implied

column5: Abraha, the Abyssinian ruler of Yemen, marched with elephants to destroy the Ka'bah but was defeated by birds dropping stones of baked clay.
column8: Historical event c.570 CE recorded in Arabian traditions; possibly refers to smallpox outbreak
column9: Unique to Islamic tradition; historically documented attempt by Abraha to attack Mecca around the time of Prophet Muhammad's birth

column5: Jonah left his people in anger, was swallowed by a whale, glorified Allah from its belly, was forgiven and cast ashore, and his people ultimately believed.
column8: May reflect Near Eastern traditions about Nineveh
column9: Quran: Presents Jonah's leaving as impatience not disobedience; specific praise formula mentioned (La ilaha illa anta, subhanaka inni kuntu min al-zalimin); less detail about Nineveh's repentance; no mention of Jonah's displeasure at God's mercy

column5: Job was afflicted with disease and loss of wealth and family but remained patient. He eventually called to Allah who restored his health, family, and wealth.
column8: May reflect broader Near Eastern traditions about righteous suffering
column9: Quran: Much briefer account; no dialogues with friends; no divine speech; emphasis on patience and mercy; specific mention of cool water spring and herb remedy; no questioning of divine justice

column5: A righteous king who traveled to the far west and east, establishing justice, and built a barrier between two mountains to protect people from Gog and Magog.
column6: Possible connection to Avestan figure Kavi Haosravah
column8: Alexander Romance traditions; Syriac Alexander Legend
column9: Unique synthesis in Quranic narrative; traditionally associated with Alexander the Great though not named explicitly; emphasizes monotheistic faith of the king; barrier against Gog and Magog more developed than Biblical mention

column5: A man (identified in tradition as Uzair) passed by a ruined town and wondered how Allah would resurrect it. Allah caused him to die for 100 years, then revived him to witness resurrection firsthand.
column7: Book of Ezra (though story not present there); some parallels to Nehemiah viewing Jerusalem
column8: May reflect Jewish traditions about Ezra
column9: Quran: Doesn't name the man explicitly as Ezra in the text; focuses on resurrection demonstration; includes food and drink preservation; emphasizes personal experience of resurrection

column1: The Parable of the Two Gardens
column5: Two men, one with two lush gardens who became arrogant and ungrateful, and another who reminded him of Allah. The ungrateful man's gardens were destroyed.
column7: Some thematic similarity to Biblical parables of rich fools
column8: Possible reflection of Arabian moral traditions
column9: Unique to Quranic narrative

column5: Allah is described as the Light of the heavens and earth, likened to a lamp in a glass, the glass like a brilliant star, lit from a blessed olive tree.
column6: Some similar imagery found in Zoroastrian light symbolism
column8: Neoplatonic conceptions of divine light; possible influence from Eastern Christian light theology
column9: Unique Quranic synthesis of light imagery as divine metaphor

column5: Those who take protectors other than Allah are likened to a spider taking a web as a home, which is the weakest of homes.
column7: Similar metaphorical structure to some Biblical parables
column8: Common natural observation adapted as spiritual metaphor
column9: Unique to Quranic narrative; uses natural observation as symbolic teaching

column5: A man given Allah's signs but who rejected them is compared to a dog that pants whether left alone or driven away.
column7: Some parallels to NT metaphors about return to sin (2 Peter 2:22)
column8: Common cultural observation turned metaphorical
column9: Unique Quranic parable; distinctive use of dog behavior as metaphor for one who rejects guidance

column5: Maryam's birth, dedication to temple, miraculous sustenance, angelic visitation, virgin birth of Jesus, and defense against accusations.
column7: Gospel infancy narratives (Matthew, Luke); Protevangelium of James
column8: May reflect Eastern Christian traditions about Mary
column9: Quran: More details about Mary's birth and upbringing; dedication to temple; Zachariah as guardian; miraculous food provision; retreat to eastern place for childbirth; speaking infant Jesus defends her honor; no Joseph mentioned

column5: Jesus' miraculous birth, speaking from the cradle, performing miracles, gathering disciples, ascension to heaven, and future return.
column8: Apocryphal Christian texts (e.g., Infancy Gospel of Thomas)
column9: Quran: Jesus speaks in cradle; creates birds from clay; explicitly denies divinity; not crucified but raised to heaven; disciples called "hawāriyyūn"; table from heaven sent as sign; no resurrection described

column1: The Story of Zakariya and Yahya
column5: Zakariya's old-age prayer for an heir, miraculous birth of Yahya, and Yahya's righteousness and prophethood.
column8: May reflect broader Near Eastern traditions
column9: Quran: Zakariya rendered temporarily mute; Yahya given wisdom as child; ascetic qualities emphasized; name "Yahya" specified as unique; no connection to baptism or Jesus' ministry described

column1: The Parable of the Good Word
column5: A good word is compared to a good tree with firm roots and branches reaching to the sky, bearing fruit in all seasons.
column8: Common agricultural metaphor in Middle Eastern context
column9: Unique Quranic development; emphasis on word/speech rather than person; more elaborate tree description

column5: People who violated the Sabbath by fishing when prohibited were transformed into apes as punishment for their transgression.
column7: Biblical Sabbath laws (Exodus 20:8-11); no transformation in Bible
column8: May reflect Midrashic or Talmudic traditions
column9: Quran: Transformation into apes as punishment; specific mention of fishing as violation; town by the sea setting; stratagem to catch fish on prohibited day

column5: Those who were entrusted with the Torah but failed to uphold it are likened to a donkey carrying books without understanding them.
column7: Similar metaphorical structure to some Biblical parables
column8: Common cultural observation as metaphor
column9: Distinctive Quranic metaphor; focus on knowledge without implementation

column1: The Parable of the Blind and Deaf
column5: One who was spiritually dead and given life (faith) is contrasted with one walking in darkness, like comparing the seeing to the blind.

column8: Common metaphorical framework in Near Eastern religious thought
column9: Unique Quranic formulation; emphasis on contrast between guidance and misguidance

column4: Compares hypocrites to those who kindle a fire that is then extinguished

"Allah is the ally of those who believe. He brings them out from darknesses into the light. And those who disbelieve - their allies are Taghut. They take them out of the light into darknesses."

column4: Describes the Quran as a light from Allah

"Praise is [due] to Allah, who created the heavens and the earth and made the darkness and the light."

column4: Compares faith to being given light to walk among people

The example of His light is like a niche within which is a lamp, the lamp is within glass, the glass as if it were a pearly [white] star lit from [the oil of] a blessed olive tree, neither of the east nor of the west, whose oil would almost glow even if untouched by fire. column3:
• Worship centered on celestial bodies (sun, moon, Venus)
• Development of stone sanctuaries (betyls) throughout Arabia
• Early forms of pilgrimage established to sacred sites

• Archaeological evidence suggests continuous settlement in Mecca Valley
• Location likely sacred due to Zamzam well, a crucial water source

• Kingdom of Saba (Sheba) develops sophisticated astral religion
• Temples to Almaqah (moon god) become important pilgrimage centers
• Development of incense trade routes connecting Arabia to Mediterranean

• Nabataean civilization develops stone sanctuaries (including Petra)
• Dushara worshipped, often represented by cubic stone blocks
• Architectural/ritualistic influence on later Meccan practices

• Islamic tradition holds Adam constructed the first sanctuary
• Ibrahim (Abraham) and Ismail rebuilt it as monotheistic shrine
• The Black Stone (al-Ḥajar al-Aswad) delivered by Angel Gabriel

• Persian Sasanian Empire (224-651 CE) spread Zoroastrianism to eastern Arabia
• Concepts of cosmic dualism (good vs. evil), judgment, and paradise
• Fire temples established in eastern Arabian regions
• Influenced concepts of purity, angelology, and eschatology

• Byzantine influence brought Christianity to northern Arabia
• Nestorian Christianity spread via trade routes from Syria
• Monastic communities established in northern Arabia
• Ethiopian (Aksumite) Christian influence in Yemen
• Christian concepts of monotheism, divine revelation, and prophets

• Established settlements in Yathrib (Medina), Khaybar, and Yemen
• Introduced strict monotheism and scriptural tradition
• Influenced concepts of dietary laws, ritual purity, and prophetic tradition

• Pre-Islamic monotheists rejecting polytheism
• Claimed to follow "religion of Abraham" (millat Ibrahim)
• Notable figures include Zayd ibn Amr and Waraqah ibn Nawfal

• The Kaaba becomes a pan-Arabian pilgrimage site
• Houses approximately 360 idols representing tribal deities
• Major deities: Hubal (chief deity), Al-Lat, Al-Uzza, Manat ("daughters of Allah")
• Other deities: Isaf, Na'ila, Wadd, Suwa', Yaghuth, Ya'uq, Nasr
• Contains paintings of Jesus, Mary, Abraham (syncretism)

• Born into the Quraysh tribe, custodians of the Kaaba
• Raised in polytheistic environment but later exposed to monotheistic ideas

• Kaaba damaged by floods; tribes collaborate to rebuild
• Dispute over Black Stone placement resolved by Muhammad
• Muhammad places stone on cloth for tribal leaders to share honor

• Muhammad receives first revelations in Cave of Hira
• Early messages emphasize strict monotheism (tawhid)
• Criticizes idol worship and tribal polytheistic practices

• Muhammad and followers migrate to Medina
• Establishes first Muslim community
• Prayer direction (qibla) initially toward Jerusalem

• Prayer direction changed from Jerusalem to the Kaaba
• Reestablishes Abraham's connection to the sacred site
• Signifies Islam's distinct identity while maintaining Abrahamic roots

• Muhammad's forces enter Mecca peacefully
• Removes all idols from the Kaaba (estimated 360+)
• Destroys paintings except possibly those of Jesus & Mary
• Reconsecrates the Kaaba to monotheistic worship
• Establishes pilgrimage rituals (Hajj) that continue today

• Four "Rightly Guided" Caliphs maintain the Kaaba's sanctity
• Standardization of Hajj rituals
• Expansion of Islamic community throughout Arabia and beyond

• Umayyad forces besiege Mecca during civil war
• Kaaba damaged during conflict
• Abdullah ibn al-Zubayr rebuilds with modifications (second door, inclusion of Hijr)

• Al-Hajjaj ibn Yusuf demolishes ibn al-Zubayr's changes
• Reverts to the Quraysh's original design
• Establishes the form that largely persists today

• Qarmatian sect raids Mecca
• Removes Black Stone to their headquarters in Al-Ahsa
• Stone returned 22 years later

• Severe floods damage the Kaaba
• Sultan Murad IV orders repairs
• Maintains foundational structure while reinforcing building

• Interior reinforced with modern materials
• Roof replaced while maintaining traditional appearance
• Black Stone temporarily removed for cleaning and preservation
